engagement.justification: Directly addresses why a good-enough answer can beat the best by citing time, resources, and over-fullness; opens awkwardly ('is game of the idea') but stays on-task.

coherence.justification: Three numbered reasons plus an objection and rebuttal in a clear arc; minor sloppiness in the first sentence and the 'always the best' phrasing in the objection.

substance.justification: Develops a real cost/benefit distinction (time, money, things used) and handles an objection, though the points are not pushed into a sharp counterexample.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Mostly reads as native UGF; the opening fragment and 'so full' are clunky but recoverable.

engagement.justification: Prompt asks about an indicative, defeasible conditional in a procedural mode; response instead describes expected-value decision-making, touching only obliquely on defeasibility.

coherence.justification: Internally consistent essay on weighing pros and cons with loss-aversion qualification, flowing logically.

substance.justification: Identifies the cost-benefit move and a loss-aversion objection but never engages the conditional/defeasibility topic asked about.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF reads naturally throughout; light clunk around 'might make you miss out' but no breakdown.

engagement.justification: Directly engages why deals hold or fall apart, framing them as promises and analyzing fairness, expectations, and trust.

coherence.justification: Three-part structure follows logically; one tautological line ('what people think is right and what people think is right') and the response is cut off mid-sentence.

substance.justification: Develops three real factors (payoff balance, outside options, trust) with mechanism, though truncation prevents full development of the trust point.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF feels mostly native; some awkward repetition but the argument flows.

engagement.justification: Prompt asks for a counterexample to a chemical-element definition; response instead discusses repetition and truth, completely off-topic.

coherence.justification: The off-topic essay itself is internally consistent and well-organized.

substance.justification: On its own terms it makes a reasonable observation about repetition not entailing truth, but it produces no counterexample and no engagement with the definition.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF reads naturally; no restriction-induced strain.

engagement.justification: Prompt asks for a counterexample to a dispositional-belief biconditional; response talks vaguely about 'saying you will do something' and what 'you get out of your doing,' missing the topic entirely.

coherence.justification: Sentences contradict and repeat ('the first part is about what you get out of your doing' twice with no progression); a near-incoherent loop.

substance.justification: Restates fragments without producing any move, counterexample, or distinction.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Words are simple but the phrasing is clunky enough that the argument never recovers.

engagement.justification: Prompt is a probabilistic-conditional modus-ponens prompt in a child-rearing setting; response instead argues against ad hominem (judging a thought by the speaker), only tangentially related.

coherence.justification: Internally consistent walk through three claims with a qualifying caveat at the end; truncated mid-sentence.

substance.justification: Develops the ad-hominem-resistance point and adds a real qualifier (the person's behavior can sometimes be evidence), but engages a different prompt.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF reads naturally; minor stilted phrasing but no breakdown.

engagement.justification: Engages the difference between a strong and weak reason, contrasting 'fits the facts' versus 'guess or feeling'; awkward opening ('that is not clear') but on-topic.

coherence.justification: Definitions are laid out, an objection is raised and answered, conclusion follows; some looseness in the 'speaker says a good reason does not make you feel sure' line which seems to contradict the rest.

substance.justification: Develops a real distinction (fit with facts, hard to break) and addresses an objection, though the analysis is shallow.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Mostly native UGF; an early fragment is the only friction.

engagement.justification: Prompt asks for a counterexample to a chemical-element definition; response talks about caring/not caring about losing money and being good or bad, entirely off-topic.

coherence.justification: First and second 'ideas' are stated as if different but read the same; final move recovers a clear conclusion ('the case does not follow').

substance.justification: Makes a logical observation about insufficient conditions but does not produce a counterexample to the actual claim.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF reads cleanly with simple words.

engagement.justification: Prompt asks for a counterexample to a translation-adequacy definition; response discusses lawmakers picking numbers, fully off-topic.

coherence.justification: Some flow but the 'and only if and only if' opening is broken and the argument jumps between not-having-to-pick and having-to-pick a number.

substance.justification: Makes a vague case for clear numerical thresholds in law; no counterexample produced.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Words are simple but the repeated 'and only if' and clunky grammar drag readability.

engagement.justification: Prompt is an indicative-conditional constructive-dilemma prompt in deontology; response is the now-familiar repetition-and-truth essay, missing the topic.

coherence.justification: Three clear ideas, with an illustrative example (sky/green) and a clean conclusion; tightly organized.

substance.justification: Develops a real epistemic point with an analogy; nothing about conditionals or dilemma logic.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Reads as native UGF throughout.

engagement.justification: Prompt asks for a literal-similarity analogy in philosophy of mind; response gives a generic expected-value/decision essay, missing the analogy task entirely.

coherence.justification: Opens with a fragment and shifts between 'the numbers we get are the same' and 'pick the best choice,' with murky transitions; the 'big picture' move is loosely tied.

substance.justification: Touches on expected-value reasoning but never produces an analogy or a developed distinction.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Mostly recoverable UGF; some stilted phrasing.

engagement.justification: Engages with whether character decides if a point is right, but the phrasing keeps wobbling between 'the point' and 'the person who picks,' partly missing the ad hominem framing.

coherence.justification: Sentences are tangled and partly contradictory ('the point is still right no matter how the point looks' alongside 'the point and the person can change whether they are right'); final sentence self-undermines.

substance.justification: There is a real move (separating the merit of the point from the speaker) but it is muddled by inconsistent reference.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF restricts feel passable, but the overloaded use of 'point' for 'argument' and 'speaker' creates real ambiguity.

engagement.justification: Prompt is about virtue-vs-rules ethics; response talks about a 'person who runs the show of what he says' with self-referential confusion, only obliquely engaging character.

coherence.justification: Repeats 'the person who runs the show of what he says' to the point of incoherence; the conclusion (X matters more than X) is essentially tautological.

substance.justification: Hints at character vs. rule-following but cannot develop the distinction because the referents collapse.

expressive_adequacy.justification: The UGF circumlocution for the role becomes unparseable; restriction effectively breaks the argument.

engagement.justification: Prompt is material-conditional/paradoxical in inductive context; response gives a clear Gettier-style essay, off-topic.

coherence.justification: Each step follows: true belief from bad reason fails knowing; analogy of lucky guess; clean conclusion.

substance.justification: Develops the canonical Gettier distinction (justified vs. lucky true belief) with an analogy, even though it misses the prompt.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Reads as native UGF, with 'strong hold on the truth' as a tidy paraphrase of justification.

engagement.justification: Directly addresses what makes a good reason for belief, listing several criteria.

coherence.justification: Opens with a fragment ('is true is that...') but the list and conclusion line up; one criterion (trusted person) is asserted without setup.

substance.justification: Develops multiple substantive criteria (fits observation, checkable, not just feeling, robust to reframing), though without examples.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Mostly natural UGF; the fragmentary opening is the only friction.

engagement.justification: Prompt asks for a counterexample to a behavior-instinctiveness definition; response is the repetition/truth essay again, fully off-topic.

coherence.justification: Tight three-step argument with the sky-green analogy and a coherent conclusion.

substance.justification: Develops the epistemic point with an analogy; no counterexample produced and no engagement with the definition.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Reads natively in UGF.

engagement.justification: Prompt asks for an anomaly analogy in mechanical/repair; response gives a clean framing-effects essay (lives-saved vs. lives-lost), unrelated.

coherence.justification: Well-organized example, contrast, objection, and conclusion; only the cut-off final sentence drops a star.

substance.justification: Develops a concrete framing example with numbers, addresses an objection, and draws a clear normative conclusion.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF feels native throughout.

engagement.justification: Prompt asks for a literal-similarity analogy in analytical style; response is a clean essay on question wording shaping answers, off-topic.

coherence.justification: Each step flows from the last, building from claim to consequence to evaluation; truncated mid-final-sentence.

substance.justification: Develops a real move (loaded questions distort answers) but no analogy or counterexample given.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Reads naturally in UGF.

engagement.justification: Prompt asks for a counterexample to a perception-guides-action biconditional; response defends absolute moral prohibitions, completely off-topic.

coherence.justification: Internally consistent deontological argument with an objection acknowledged; some looseness in the 'two bad things at the same time' wording.

substance.justification: Develops a real deontological point (people as ends, not tools) with the trolley-style case, though no counterexample is given.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Mostly natural UGF; 'as a thing to get a good end' is slightly stilted but workable.

engagement.justification: Prompt asks for a counterexample to a sovereignty biconditional; response gives a Pareto-improvement-style essay, off-topic.

coherence.justification: Argument loses thread between 'cannot give one without taking from another' and 'help without hurting,' and the conclusion contradicts the first idea in places.

substance.justification: Hints at Pareto reasoning but no counterexample, distinction, or sharp move; mostly restates the prompt's hypothetical.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Words simple but the muddled logic strains the UGF prose.

engagement.justification: Prompt asks for a counterexample to a God-existence-argument biconditional; response is again the repetition-and-truth essay.

coherence.justification: Each step follows the last; the three ideas build to a clear conclusion.

substance.justification: Develops the same canned epistemic point about truth's mind-independence; thoughtful but no counterexample to the actual claim.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Reads as native UGF.

engagement.justification: Prompt asks for a counterexample to a climate-region biconditional; response argues that 'a hard word way' gives the same good thing, drifting into Pareto-style reasoning.

coherence.justification: Opens with a fragment and the argument keeps reversing itself (best way doesn't change vs. it changes); the conclusion contradicts mid-essay claims.

substance.justification: Touches on the relativity of 'best' but no counterexample to the definition and no developed distinction.

expressive_adequacy.justification: The phrase 'a hard word way' is opaque and the prose is clunky throughout.

engagement.justification: Prompt is an indicative-conditional modus-ponens in consequentialist context; response discusses repeated game cooperation, only loosely related.

coherence.justification: Three-part structure flows; the pros/cons distinction is laid out but a few qualifiers are awkwardly stated.

substance.justification: Develops a real point (shadow of the future encourages cooperation) plus a genuine caveat (free-riding risk, over-niceness); no engagement with conditional logic.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Reads naturally in UGF; light awkwardness around 'make a way that says.'

engagement.justification: Directly engages the one-vs-many harm problem, framing it as a tension between two ethical views.

coherence.justification: Tight introduction, two opposed views laid out symmetrically, a clear statement of the conflict; the cut-off ending is the only minor drop.

substance.justification: Develops both consequentialist and deontological sides with reasoning, though the trade-off itself is not resolved or pushed further (likely truncated).

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF reads as native; 'use a person like a thing' is a clean translation of instrumentalization.

engagement.justification: Directly engages whose good matters and how much by canvassing three ethical frameworks.

coherence.justification: Three clearly demarcated views, each introduced with structure; truncated mid third view but each step is clean.

substance.justification: Develops consequentialism and deontology with concrete examples; third view (care ethics?) is cut off before substance.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Reads as native UGF, with clear and apt simple-word substitutions for technical terms.

engagement.justification: Prompt asks for a counterexample to a climate-region biconditional; response is the same repetition-and-truth essay.

coherence.justification: Tightly organized three-step argument, with a clean conclusion.

substance.justification: Develops the epistemic point well, but no counterexample to the prompt.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Reads as native UGF.

engagement.justification: Directly engages how to think about another person's reaction before acting; three concrete steps.

coherence.justification: Clear sequence (the thing, the other's interests, the interaction, their interpretation) with each step building on the prior; cut off mid synthesis sentence.

substance.justification: Develops perspective-taking and second-order modeling ('what they think about what you want') with concrete consequences; no rich example.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF reads as native; 'see the world through their eyes' is a clean idiom-preserving phrase.

engagement.justification: Prompt asks for a counterexample to a cooperation biconditional; response gives an abstract essay about 'first thing' implying 'second thing,' off-topic.

coherence.justification: Internally consistent walk through three abstract ideas with a defensible conclusion; very abstract referents weaken clarity.

substance.justification: Identifies a methodological point (check the implication holds across cases) but never instantiates it; no counterexample.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF passable; the lack of concrete referents (everything is 'the first thing') is clunky.

engagement.justification: Prompt asks for a literal-similarity analogy in philosophy of mind; response gives a Gettier-style essay (red ball example), off-topic but with mild relevance to mind/knowledge.

coherence.justification: Each step follows: true belief, bad-reason qualifier, justified-true-belief synthesis; truncated.

substance.justification: Develops the classic Gettier-fit point with an example (red ball) and a real qualifier.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Reads as native UGF; 'good reason that fits with the world' is a deft simple-word rendering of justification.

engagement.justification: Directly engages why rule-following without care for people is insufficient, with a concrete hypothetical.

coherence.justification: Each step follows: define rules, define caring, give a tension case, draw a normative conclusion; truncated mid-final sentence.

substance.justification: Develops a real distinction with a sketched case; the case is general rather than vivid but the point lands.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Reads as native UGF; 'words on paper' and 'no heart' are clean simple-word analogues.
